context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m stuck on the climate action plan prospectus rn. idk how to turn my city research into 2 actual policy components.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — let's work with what you already have. What city did you pick, and what were the main vulnerabilities or climate risks you identified in your research so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and feeling stuck on how to translate research into policy components. I should not scaffold too much — instead, I'll ask them to share what they already have (their city, SWOT analysis, barriers) so I can help them build from there using guided discovery. This keeps ownership with the student and gives me a baseline.

exchanges.student: i picked Jakarta, and rn i mostly have flooding, extreme heat, and land subsidence. i’m not sure which of those should become the 2 components tho.
exchanges.tutor: Those are all relevant risks. The assignment asks you to think about EJ implications and interactions between components. With that in mind — do any of those three risks overlap with each other, or disproportionately affect specific communities in Jakarta?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified three solid climate risks. Rather than telling them which two to pick, I should use guided discovery to help them think through the selection criteria themselves — specifically nudging them toward the assignment's emphasis on Environmental Justice, overlaps/synergies between components, and actionability. A single question pointing them toward the assignment's own criteria is the right move here.

exchanges.student: yeah i think flooding and subsidence overlap a lot, esp in poorer coastal areas. heat prob hits low-income neighborhoods too, but i’m kinda fuzzy on which 2 make the strongest pair.
exchanges.tutor: You're already thinking in the right direction. The assignment specifically asks about interactions and synergies between your two components. So ask yourself: which pair would give you the most to say about how one policy supports the other?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a good connection between flooding/subsidence and EJ. They're close to making the decision themselves. Rather than confirming which pair is best, I should ask them to think about what 'strongest pair' means in the context of this assignment — specifically the 'interactions' section which asks about synergies between components. One targeted question should get them there.

exchanges.student: maybe flooding + subsidence, since they’re kinda linked? but idk if that feels too repetitive.
exchanges.tutor: Being linked isn't the same as being repetitive — the assignment actually rewards synergies. The question is whether you can propose two *distinct* policy responses, even if the problems overlap. Can you think of a concrete policy intervention for each one separately?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is second-guessing a good instinct. They're conflating 'linked' with 'repetitive,' which is a misconception worth addressing critically but briefly. The synergy between components is actually a feature of the assignment, not a flaw. I should push back lightly and redirect them to think about the difference between two problems being related vs. two policies being distinct.

exchanges.student: uh maybe one is coastal flood protection, and the other is reducing groundwater extraction? i’m not fully sure if that second one is framed right tho.
exchanges.tutor: Those are promising directions. Before worrying about framing, think about this: for each one, who are the most affected communities, and what's the specific opportunity for action in Jakarta's current situation? That'll help you build your 'Context' and 'Opportunity' sections.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has proposed two distinct policy directions that are actually quite strong. Rather than validating or correcting them directly, I should push them to think about whether those interventions address the assignment's required components — specifically context, opportunity, and EJ implications. This moves the conversation forward toward actual writing rather than staying in planning mode.

exchanges.student: ok yeah, for flood protection it’d be coastal kampungs and low-income residents in flood-prone districts. for groundwater, maybe the opportunity is expanding piped water so ppl don’t have to rely on wells?
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've got the core ideas for both components now. You've been planning well; it's time to start drafting. Try writing your 'Context' section for one of the two components first. What's the current situation and key challenge in Jakarta for that policy area?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making solid connections between the policy interventions and EJ implications, and has identified a concrete opportunity for the groundwater/subsidence component. This is good progress. We've been in planning mode for a few exchanges now — it's a good moment to push them toward actually drafting rather than continuing to discuss. I should acknowledge the progress and prompt them to start writing.

exchanges.student: ok lemme try: Jakarta has severe flooding worsened by sea level rise, heavy rainfall, and land subsidence, and low-income coastal communities face the biggest risks. key challenge is that flooding isn’t just one cause, so city response gets fragmented.
exchanges.tutor: Good start — the multi-causal framing is smart. But 'fragmented response' is vague. What does fragmented actually look like in Jakarta's case? Who are the actors involved, and what specifically breaks down?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a decent first attempt at a Context section. It's concise and identifies the multi-causal nature of flooding as the key challenge, which is accurate. However, it could be stronger — 'fragmented response' is a bit vague and could be more specific. I should acknowledge what works and push them to sharpen the key challenge without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: yeah that’s where i get stuck tbh. i think it’s like city gov, national gov, and utilities all doing diff stuff, but i can’t say it cleanly yet.
exchanges.tutor: You have the actors right. Now think about the consequence: what actually happens to flood-affected communities when those actors aren't coordinated?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right idea but is struggling to articulate it clearly. I should not write it for them. A targeted question about the consequence of that fragmentation — rather than the fragmentation itself — might help them find cleaner language on their own.

exchanges.student: i think projects end up uneven or delayed, and poorer areas get less protection first. so the breakdown is not just admin stuff, it turns into unequal flood risk, right?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — that's your EJ thread right there. Now go back and revise your Context section to include that consequence. You have all the pieces.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just made a strong conceptual leap — connecting administrative fragmentation to unequal flood risk. This is exactly the EJ angle the assignment calls for. I should affirm this briefly and push them to revise their Context section incorporating this insight, moving toward actual written output.

exchanges.student: ok, i’d revise it to say Jakarta’s flooding is driven by sea level rise, heavy rainfall, and subsidence, but responses are split across city agencies, national projects, and water infrastructure systems. bc of that, protection is uneven and low-income coastal communities can end up with higher flood exposure and slower recovery.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid revision — the connection between governance fragmentation and unequal exposure is clear and relevant. Move on to your 'Opportunity' section now: what's the specific opening for action in Jakarta that this policy could take advantage of?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a strong revised Context section that integrates the multi-causal framing, governance fragmentation, and EJ implications cleanly. This is good progress. I should acknowledge it briefly and move them forward to the next section — 'Opportunity' — to keep momentum going and avoid dwelling on what's already solid.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. All key ideas — the EJ thread, the governance fragmentation framing, the two policy components — were generated by the student through guided questioning. The tutor never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them, never rewrote student text, and never gave submission-ready content. Scaffolding was well-sequenced, building from vulnerability identification to component selection to drafting to revision. Responses were concise and targeted. No deductions were warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions were applied across any criterion. The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning to guide the student rather than providing answers (Turns 2–5), built on student progress at each step (Turns 6–10), and gave specific process-level feedback when the student's draft was vague (Turn 7: 'fragmented response is vague — what does fragmented actually look like?'). The tutor never produced submission-ready text, never rewrote student sentences, and never revealed conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. Responses were appropriately brief and interactive throughout, and tone was consistently supportive without implying formal grading.
